papers.title: Monodisperse PEG engineering for quantifiable surface conjugation on PLGA nanoparticles.
papers.abstract: Surface PEGylation is a well-established strategy to enhance the stability, functionality, and bioavailability of nanoparticles (NPs) in biomedical applications. However, the widespread use of polydisperse PEG derivatives limits the ability to precisely quantify surface PEGylation efficiency (mol%) and evaluate its effect on NP properties. In this study, we present a systematic approach to engineer PLGA NPs using structurally defined monodisperse PEG-diamine derivatives of varying chain lengths (PEG6, PEG26, PEG45), synthesized and purified by chromatographic methods and covalently attached vi
papers.summary: This paper presents a method to engineer PLGA nanoparticles with precisely defined monodisperse PEG chains, enabling accurate quantification of surface PEGylation efficiency. This approach overcomes limitations of traditional polydisperse PEG, allowing better control and evaluation of nanoparticle properties for biomedical applications.

papers.title: Mitochondrial Voltage-Dependent Anion Channel: From a Passive Pore to a Cellular Hub Through Protein Complexation.
papers.abstract: The voltage-dependent anion channel (VDAC) is the primary conduit for ion and metabolite transport across the mitochondrial outer membrane. Positioned at the interface between the cytosol and the mitochondrial compartment, VDAC is uniquely accessible to proteins on both sides of the membrane, making it an interaction hub whose biophysical properties and signaling functions are shaped by protein complexation in addition to its intrinsic pore specialization. Mammals express three isoforms-VDAC1, VDAC2, and VDAC3-sharing a conserved β-barrel scaffold with about 70% identity. However, minor differ
papers.summary: This review discusses how the voltage-dependent anion channel (VDAC) in mitochondria is not just a passive pore but a dynamic hub whose functions are regulated by interactions with other proteins. It highlights the three mammalian VDAC isoforms and how their biophysical properties and signaling roles are shaped by protein complexation.

papers.title: Integrated Screening Cascades for Ion-Channel Drug Discovery: Linking Structure, Electrophysiology, Safety Pharmacology, and Human-Relevant Models.
papers.abstract: Ion channels are validated drug targets, but they remain difficult to study as their pharmacology is influenced by rapid gating, conformational state transitions, cell-type-specific expression, and narrow safety margins. Recent advances in cryo-electron microscopy, structure-based in silico screening, machine-learning-guided prioritization, optical high-throughput screening, automated patch-clamp electrophysiology, and human-relevant organoid or microphysiological system (MPS) models are transforming this field. In this expanded review, we examine how these modalities can be integrated into a
papers.summary: This review discusses how integrating cryo-EM, machine learning, automated patch-clamp, and human organoid models can overcome challenges in ion-channel drug discovery, such as rapid gating and safety margins. It highlights recent technological advances that enable more efficient and human-relevant screening cascades for these difficult but validated drug targets.

papers.title: In Silico Design of Pyrimidine Derivatives as Potential α-Glucosidase Inhibitors: QSAR, Molecular Docking, ADMET, and Molecular Dynamics Studies.
papers.abstract: Diabetes mellitus remains a major metabolic disorder requiring the development of new and effective α-glucosidase inhibitors. The present study aimed to identify, design, and optimize novel 3-amino-2,4-diarylbenzo[4,5]imidazo[1,2-α]pyrimidine derivatives with promising inhibitory activity against the α-glucosidase enzyme using a comprehensive in silico strategy. Approximately 300 molecular descriptors were calculated to characterize a dataset of 32 compounds (Peytam et al.) and to investigate the structural factors governing their biological activity. Based on these descriptors, a multiple lin
papers.summary: This study used computational methods to design new pyrimidine derivatives as potential α-glucosidase inhibitors for diabetes treatment. By analyzing molecular descriptors and performing QSAR, docking, and dynamics simulations, the researchers identified promising compounds for further development.

papers.title: Structure-Guided Discovery and Biochemical Validation of Novel Small-Molecule Inhibitors Predicted to Target the CCHFV OTU Protease Y89-W99 Pocket.
papers.abstract: Crimean-Congo hemorrhagic fever virus (CCHFV) remains a major public health threat due to its high mortality rates and the absence of approved antiviral therapies. The viral ovarian tumor (OTU) protease is a critical virulence factor that suppresses host innate immunity through its deubiquitinase activity, making it an attractive therapeutic target. In this study, we employed a structure-guided approach to identify and validate novel small-molecule inhibitors targeting the non-catalytic Y89-W99 pocket of the OTU protease. Recombinant OTU protease was successfully expressed, purified, and refol
papers.summary: Researchers used a structure-guided approach to discover small-molecule inhibitors targeting a non-catalytic pocket of the Crimean-Congo hemorrhagic fever virus OTU protease, a key virulence factor. They expressed and purified the recombinant protease to validate these novel inhibitors, which could lead to new antiviral therapies for this deadly disease.

papers.title: Effects of grinding penetration depth and abrasive grain spacing on atomic interactions and material removal mechanisms in silicon during ultrasonic vibration-assisted grinding: a molecular dynamics study.
papers.abstract: Grinding is an important finishing process for hard, brittle materials like silicon, where atomic-level finishing is critical for semiconductor and microelectronic applications. Ultrasonic vibration-assisted grinding has been shown to enhance grinding efficiency beyond conventional methods, but several of its atomic-level mechanisms remain incompletely understood. The interaction among the following was investigated in this study: mean abrasive grain spacing, grinding penetration depth, and abrasive grain physical properties. Atomic interactions and behaviors of the grinding process were model
papers.summary: This study uses molecular dynamics simulations to investigate how ultrasonic vibration-assisted grinding affects atomic interactions and material removal in silicon. The researchers examined the effects of abrasive grain spacing and grinding penetration depth on the grinding process at the atomic level. The findings provide insights into the mechanisms that make ultrasonic vibration-assisted grinding more efficient than conventional methods for finishing hard, brittle materials like silicon.

papers.title: Decoding dry eye disease based on bioinformatics and in vitro experimental: the role of immune responses and natural product intervention.
papers.abstract: Dry eye disease (DED), a prevalent ocular condition, has seen rising incidence rates. Aberrant inflammation and immune dysregulation are key pathogenic factors in DED. However, the underlying mechanisms linking autoimmunity to DED therapy remain incompletely understood. Herbal medicine's potential in treating DED warrants further exploration due to rendering monotherapy insufficient for clinical needs.
papers.summary: This study uses bioinformatics and in vitro experiments to investigate the role of immune responses in dry eye disease (DED) and explores the potential of natural products as therapeutic interventions. The findings highlight key immune-related mechanisms and suggest that herbal compounds may offer new treatment options for DED.

papers.title: Enhancing virtual screening of cystathionine β-synthase inhibitors: benchmarking target-specific machine-learning scoring functions against state-of-the-art AI docking and co-folding approaches.
papers.abstract: Cystathionine β-synthase (CBS) has emerged as an important therapeutic target implicated in cancer and Down syndrome, yet the discovery of selective CBS inhibitors remains challenging due to limited structural diversity of known ligands and the scarcity of target-focused virtual screening (VS) benchmarks. In this study, we present the first comprehensive evaluation of CBS-specific artificial intelligence (AI) models for structure-based VS, supported by a carefully curated and up-to-date data set of experimentally validated CBS inhibitors, true inactives and decoys. Using this data set, we deve
papers.summary: This study benchmarks machine-learning scoring functions and AI docking methods for identifying inhibitors of cystathionine β-synthase (CBS), a therapeutic target in cancer and Down syndrome. The authors created a curated dataset of validated CBS inhibitors and decoys, and found that target-specific ML models outperform general AI docking and co-folding approaches for virtual screening. This provides a useful benchmark for CBS drug discovery.

papers.title: Single cell and bulk transcriptomics reveal metabolic reprogramming biomarker signatures during extracorporeal membrane oxygenation for cardiogenic shock.
papers.abstract: Cardiogenic shock (CS) patients receiving extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO) exhibit profound immune and metabolic disturbances, which may influence early clinical outcomes. However, the transcriptomic features linking metabolic reprogramming, immune-cell alterations, and early outcomes after ECMO initiation remain incompletely understood. The GSE182600 dataset from the public database was analyzed to identify differentially expressed genes (DEGs) between ECMO-treated CS samples with successful and failed outcomes. Multiple machine learning algorithms and the Shapley Additive exPlanati
papers.summary: This study analyzes blood transcriptomes from cardiogenic shock patients on ECMO to identify gene expression differences between those who survive and those who do not. Using machine learning, the authors pinpoint metabolic reprogramming and immune-cell-related biomarkers that may predict early outcomes. The findings suggest that metabolic and immune disturbances detectable in blood could serve as prognostic signatures for ECMO therapy.

papers.title: SEVA: structural and evolutionary feature integration for predicting virulence factors and antibiotic resistance genes.
papers.abstract: Infectious diseases continue to pose unprecedented challenges to public health and the global economy. Virulence factors (VFs) enable pathogens to adhere, reproduce, and cause damage to host cells, while antibiotic resistance genes (ARGs) enable pathogens to withstand treatments that would otherwise be effective. The concurrent identification of VFs and ARGs is crucial for efficient pathogen surveillance. However, existing tools for predicting VFs or ARGs typically suffer from high false negative rates and limitations in identifying only high-identity genes against known reference VF or ARG da
papers.summary: This paper presents SEVA, a new computational method that integrates structural and evolutionary features to simultaneously predict virulence factors and antibiotic resistance genes from microbial genomes. The approach addresses the high false negative rates of existing tools by moving beyond simple sequence identity matching to capture more distant evolutionary relationships.

papers.title: A lightweight, integrated generative AI assistant for accelerated early-stage drug discovery on constrained-resource hardware.
papers.abstract: Discovery of novel therapeutic small molecules remains one of the most challenging tasks in pharmaceutical research, as it is described by high attrition rates, well over $2 billion per candidate, and timelines often surpassing a decade. Generative AI has transformative potential to probe the enormous chemical space estimated at 1060 drug-like molecules, but current computational approaches are fragmented across multiple tools and require enterprise-grade hardware, thus creating a sort of "computational divide" that excludes many academic groups and smaller laboratories. We introduce in this s
papers.summary: This paper presents a lightweight, integrated generative AI assistant designed to run on constrained-resource hardware, aiming to democratize early-stage drug discovery for academic groups and smaller labs that lack enterprise-grade computing. The tool consolidates multiple computational approaches into a single platform to efficiently explore the vast chemical space of drug-like molecules.

papers.title: Elucidating the mechanism of Buyang Huanwu Decoction in the treatment of ischemic stroke: A network pharmacology and molecular docking study.
papers.abstract: A large number of functional disorders and uncomfortable symptoms often remain following ischemic stroke (IS). Existing drug therapy is not ideal for the direct improvement of symptoms, which often leads to poor patient compliance with physical rehabilitation therapy. Buyang Huanwu Decoction (BYHWD) is a famous prescription that is effective in treating IS, especially during the sequela stage of IS. We analyzed the therapeutic mechanism of BYHWD through network pharmacology. This study aims to investigate the potential active ingredients, targets, and signaling pathways of BYHWD for the treatm
papers.summary: This study uses network pharmacology and molecular docking to identify potential active ingredients and targets of Buyang Huanwu Decoction for treating ischemic stroke. The analysis suggests the decoction may act through multiple signaling pathways to improve symptoms, particularly in the sequela stage. However, the findings are computational and require experimental validation.

papers.title: Mechanistic study on comorbidities of kidney stones and sepsis using transcriptome sequencing combined with single-cell spatial transcriptome sequencing.
papers.abstract: Kidney stones and sepsis have a complex pathological association. Urinary obstruction and infection caused by kidney stones can easily induce sepsis, leading to increased mortality and poor prognosis. However, the molecular mechanism underlying their comorbidity remains unclear, and there is a lack of specific biomarkers for early prediction and targeted therapy. This study aims to explore the molecular regulatory network of their comorbidity, screen key genes, and clarify their roles in disease progression. Transcriptome, single-cell ribonucleic acid sequencing (scRNA-seq), and spatial transc
papers.summary: This study investigates the molecular mechanisms linking kidney stones and sepsis by analyzing transcriptome, single-cell RNA, and spatial transcriptome data. It identifies key genes and regulatory networks that may serve as biomarkers for early prediction and targeted therapy of this comorbidity.

papers.title: Co-production of high-purity floridoside and isofloridoside ameliorates MASH via Parabacteroides goldsteinii-UDCA-FXR enterohepatic axis.
papers.abstract: Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH), the progressive form of metabolic dysfunction-associated fatty liver disease (MAFLD), is tightly linked to gut microbiota dysbiosis and disrupted bile acid (BA) homeostasis. Floridoside (Flor), a marine glycoside from the edible seaweed Pyropia haitanensis (P. haitanensis), exerts promising biological activities. However, protocols for its high-purity preparation and the mechanisms underlying its anti-MASH effects remain unclear.
papers.summary: This study developed a method to co-produce high-purity floridoside and isofloridoside from seaweed and showed that these compounds ameliorate metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH) in mice by modulating the gut microbiota, specifically increasing Parabacteroides goldsteinii, which in turn alters bile acid metabolism via the UDCA-FXR enterohepatic axis. The findings provide a new marine-derived therapeutic candidate for MASH and clarify its mechanism of action involving the gut-liver axis.

papers.title: Proteomic identification of NF-κB-related targets associated with the anti-inflammatory activity of Gomisin R in LPS-stimulated RAW264.7 cells.
papers.abstract: Kadsura longipedunculata Finet et Gagnep. belongs to the Kadsura Kaempf.exJuss. Vaccinium of the family Magnoliaceae. It mainly distributes in regions such as Guangxi, Sichuan, Fujian and Yunnan of China. Commonly used as traditional Chinese medicine for anti-inflammation and analgesia, it has a long history in folk medicine. In recent years, research on the active components and pharmacological activities of K. longipedunculata Finet et Gagnep. has received extensive attention. Many studies have reported that lignans are the characteristic active components, among which the gomisin group of c
papers.summary: This study identifies proteins regulated by Gomisin R, a lignan from Kadsura longipedunculata, that are linked to its anti-inflammatory effects in immune cells. Using proteomics, the authors show that Gomisin R suppresses inflammation by targeting the NF-κB signaling pathway. The findings provide molecular insight into the traditional anti-inflammatory use of this plant compound.

papers.title: AI-assisted fragment-based drug discovery of SARS-CoV-2 macrodomain binders validated by NMR and X-ray crystallography.
papers.abstract: Fragment-based drug discovery (FBDD) is an effective approach for exploring chemical space using small, low-affinity fragments as starting points to facilitate development of lead compounds. Strategies to improve fragment potency include fragment merging and linking to generate higher-affinity inhibitors. Recently, artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) have accelerated this process through structure-based optimization and generative compound design. Here, we present an AI-assisted FBDD workflow applied to the SARS-CoV-2 macrodomain (Mac1), a conserved viral protein involved in
papers.summary: This study presents an AI-assisted fragment-based drug discovery workflow targeting the SARS-CoV-2 macrodomain, a conserved viral protein. The approach uses machine learning to optimize fragment merging and linking, leading to higher-affinity inhibitors validated by NMR and X-ray crystallography. This demonstrates how AI can accelerate the early stages of drug discovery for challenging viral targets.

papers.title: Biofabrication of Pterocarpus marsupium extract-mediated CoFe2O4 and Ag-decorated CoFe2O4 nanoparticles for antioxidant and antibacterial applications: experimental and computational validation.
papers.abstract: This study presents the green synthesis of cobalt ferrite (CoFe2O4) and extract-mediated (phytochemical-capped) CoFe2O4-Ag nanocomposites using aqueous bark extract of Pterocarpus marsupium Roxb. The silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) are uniformly decorated on the surface of CoFe2O4. The characterisation results from X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis indicated a cubic spinel structure, with a crystallite size of about 23.85 nm and Ag-induced lattice strain, yielding a lattice parameter of a 0 = 8.1662 Å. The surface plasmon resonance (SPR) peak at 368 nm in the UV-vis spectroscopy spectrum revealed
papers.summary: This study describes a green synthesis method for creating cobalt ferrite nanoparticles and silver-decorated cobalt ferrite nanocomposites using an extract from the bark of the Pterocarpus marsupium tree. The resulting nanoparticles showed antioxidant and antibacterial activity, with computational modeling supporting the experimental findings.

papers.title: Aging-driven transcriptional programs in diabetic kidney disease: multi-omics discovery of diagnostic biomarkers and drug-repurposing targets.
papers.abstract: Diabetic kidney disease (DKD) is the primary global cause of end-stage renal disease. However, the aging-related gene networks driving its progression remain unclear.
papers.summary: This study uses multi-omics data to identify aging-related gene networks that drive diabetic kidney disease progression, and discovers potential diagnostic biomarkers and drug-repurposing targets. The findings clarify how aging-related transcriptional programs contribute to DKD, offering new avenues for treatment.

papers.title: Bioinformatics identification of candidate biomarkers associated with T cell proliferation in atherosclerosis via WGCNA and machine learning.
papers.abstract: Atherosclerosis (AS) is a complex chronic disease caused by the development of atherosclerotic plaques. T cell proliferation exerts a vital influence on development of the AS. This research aimed to conduct a comprehensive analysis to computationally screen candidate T cell proliferation-related biomarkers in AS and to explore their potential molecular mechanisms.
papers.summary: This study uses bioinformatics methods (WGCNA and machine learning) to identify genes linked to T cell proliferation that may serve as biomarkers for atherosclerosis. The findings suggest specific candidate genes that could help understand the immune mechanisms driving plaque development.

papers.title: Design and immunoinformatics characterization of a novel multi-epitope vaccine rPRRSV-35N targeting NADC30-like and NADC34-like porcine reproductive and respiratory syndrome virus variants.
papers.abstract: Porcine reproductive and respiratory syndrome (PRRS), caused by the genetically diverse porcine reproductive and respiratory syndrome virus (PRRSV), remains a major economic burden to the global swine industry. Current commercial vaccines, including modified live virus (MLV) and inactivated vaccines, have shown limited effectiveness against newly emerging PRRSV variants, particularly NADC30-like and NADC34-like strains. There is therefore a need for improved vaccine strategies targeting these circulating strains. In this study, we used immunoinformatics approaches to design a multi-epitope vac
papers.summary: Researchers used computational immunoinformatics to design a multi-epitope vaccine (rPRRSV-35N) targeting two emerging, problematic strains of porcine reproductive and respiratory syndrome virus (NADC30-like and NADC34-like). This approach aims to overcome the limited effectiveness of current commercial vaccines against these genetically diverse variants. The study provides a computational design framework for a potentially more broadly protective vaccine.

papers.title: Improving the yield and biological activity of sage essential oil using NADES: in vitro and in silico insights.
papers.abstract: This study investigates the efficacy of Natural Deep Eutectic Solvents (NADESs) as innovative green pretreatments for the hydrodistillation of sage (Salvia officinalis L.) leaves, focusing on essential oil (EO) yield, phytochemical modulation, and biological activity. Three choline chloride (ChCl)-based NADESs were synthesized and characterized via ATR-FTIR. The results revealed that NADES pretreatments significantly enhanced extraction efficiency, with the ChCl: Malic acid pretreatment achieving the highest EO yield of 2.53% (v/w) compared to 2.31% for the water-based control. GC-MS analysis
papers.summary: This study shows that using natural deep eutectic solvents (NADESs) as a pretreatment for extracting sage essential oil can increase yield and improve biological activity compared to traditional water-based methods. The choline chloride-malic acid NADES gave the best results, boosting oil yield from 2.31% to 2.53% and enhancing the oil's beneficial properties.

papers.title: Integration of molecular docking, molecular dynamics simulations, and free energy calculations identifies a novel flavonoid glycoside as a PCSK9 inhibitor for hypercholesterolemia.
papers.abstract: Proprotein convertase subtilisin/kexin type 9 (PCSK9) significantly influences plasma low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C) homeostasis by promoting the degradation of LDL receptors. While monoclonal antibody therapies are effective, their widespread application is limited by high costs and invasive administration, necessitating the development of oral small-molecule inhibitors for hypercholesterolemia management. This study evaluates the therapeutic potential of a specific flavonoid glycoside, 7-(alpha-D-Glucopyranosyloxy)-5-hydroxy-2-(4-hydroxyphenyl)-4 H-1-benzopyran-4-one, as a novel
papers.summary: This study uses computational methods to identify a flavonoid glycoside compound that may inhibit PCSK9, a protein that regulates cholesterol levels. The compound was found through molecular docking and simulations to potentially bind to PCSK9, offering a possible oral small-molecule alternative to expensive antibody therapies for managing high cholesterol.

papers.title: Systems-level design of a multi-epitope immunotherapeutic vaccine targeting EBV-associated oncogenesis.
papers.abstract: Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) is an oncogenic herpesvirus associated with multiple lymphoid and epithelial malignancies. Despite extensive investigation of EBV vaccine strategies, effective therapeutic approaches capable of targeting established EBV-associated cancers remain limited. In this study, we developed an integrated immunoinformatics and structure-guided framework for the design and prioritization of therapeutic multi-epitope vaccine candidates targeting both structural glycoproteins (gp350, gB, gH/gL, and gp42) and latency-associated proteins (EBNA1, LMP1, LMP2, and BZLF1). Sixteen multi-
papers.summary: This study uses computational methods to design a multi-epitope vaccine targeting Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) proteins involved in cancer. The vaccine aims to stimulate an immune response against both structural and latency-associated EBV proteins, potentially offering a therapeutic approach for EBV-related cancers.

papers.title: Designing novel adenine clamp-CD73 mutants delineating surface expression and enzyme activity through in silico and in vitro engineering.
papers.abstract: NT5E, a 5' Ectonucleotidase, is an emerging hotspot for cancer immunotherapies. However, available enzymatic inhibitors have shown limited promise, indicating activity-independent roles. Delineating activity-dependent and independent roles of this protein with precision requisites identification of critical residues impacting activity without hampering surface expression/stability. Conserved Phenylalanine (F) at 417 and 500 positions, which form a π stack with substrate-AMP, was chosen as the region of interest for in silico mutagenesis. F417 and F500 were mutated to Valine (V) individually us
papers.summary: This study identifies key phenylalanine residues (F417 and F500) in the CD73 protein that are critical for its enzymatic activity but not for its surface expression, enabling the design of mutants that separate activity-dependent from activity-independent functions in cancer immunotherapy. Using in silico mutagenesis and experimental validation, the authors show that mutating these residues to valine reduces enzyme activity while preserving protein stability and surface localization. This approach provides a tool to precisely study CD73's roles beyond its enzymatic function, which may inform better therapeutic strategies.

papers.title: Dynamics-enhanced molecular property prediction guided by deep learning.
papers.abstract: Molecular property prediction (MPP) is a key challenge in computational biology and drug discovery. Traditional approaches mostly rely on feature representations yielded from static structures of molecules, ignoring their dynamic nature. The scarcity of dynamics data in public databases and the complexity of learning such high-dimensional data made it more difficult for dynamics-involved studies. Accordingly, we built a series of dynamics datasets for MPP tasks by performing comprehensive molecular dynamics (MD) simulations on different molecules. In addition, we proposed a dynamically enhance
papers.summary: This study addresses the challenge of molecular property prediction by incorporating molecular dynamics data, which captures the dynamic behavior of molecules rather than relying solely on static structures. The authors created new dynamics datasets through extensive molecular dynamics simulations and developed a deep learning approach that leverages this dynamic information to improve prediction accuracy. This work provides both valuable resources and a methodological framework for more realistic molecular property prediction in drug discovery.

papers.title: Mechanistic insight into signal bias by the agonist-dependent conformational dynamics of GPR84.
papers.abstract: GPR84 is an orphan class A GPCR primarily expressed in immune cells, where it plays key roles in inflammation and metabolism. Here, we present the cryo-electron microscopy structures of the GPR84-Gi complex bound to the G protein-biased agonist DL-175, and the inactive state of GPR84 bound to the antagonist GLPG1205. Combined with signaling assays and molecular dynamics simulations, these structures elucidate the conformational landscape spanning the inactive and G protein-biased active states of GPR84, providing a mechanistic basis for biased agonism. Notably, steric interactions between DL-1
papers.summary: This study uses cryo-electron microscopy to reveal the structures of the GPR84 receptor bound to a biased agonist and an antagonist, showing how different drugs can selectively activate specific signaling pathways. The findings provide a mechanistic understanding of biased agonism in this immune receptor, which could guide the development of more targeted anti-inflammatory drugs with fewer side effects.

papers.title: In silico design and validation of high-affinity RNA aptamers for SARS-CoV-2 comparable to neutralizing antibodies.
papers.abstract: Nucleic acid aptamers hold promise for clinical applications, yet understanding their molecular binding mechanisms to target proteins, and efficiently optimizing their binding affinities, remain challenging. Here, we present CAAMO (Computer-Aided Aptamer Modeling and Optimization), which integrates in silico aptamer design with experimental validation to accelerate the development of aptamer-based RNA therapeutics. Starting from the sequence information of a reported RNA aptamer, Ta, for the SARS-CoV-2 spike protein, our CAAMO method first determines its binding mode with the spike protein's r
papers.summary: This study presents CAAMO, a computational method that integrates in silico aptamer design with experimental validation to rapidly optimize RNA aptamers targeting the SARS-CoV-2 spike protein. The approach successfully generated high-affinity aptamers with binding affinities comparable to neutralizing antibodies, demonstrating a powerful pipeline for developing RNA-based therapeutics.

papers.title: Structural insights into multitargeting Mycobacterium tuberculosis Pkn kinases.
papers.abstract: Tuberculosis remains one of the leading major global health challenges, driven by the emergence of multidrug-resistant and extensively drug-resistant bacterial strains. Resistant strains complicate treatment, which often requires prolonged use of toxic second- and third-line drugs. Protein phosphorylation plays critical roles in Mycobacterium tuberculosis, with serine/threonine kinases PknA, PknB, and PknG being essential for survival, virulence, and persistence. In this study, we screened an in-house kinase inhibitor library to identify compounds targeting these kinases. Four structurally div
papers.summary: This study identifies compounds that target three essential protein kinases (PknA, PknB, PknG) in Mycobacterium tuberculosis, offering potential leads for new tuberculosis drugs. The structural insights into how these inhibitors bind could help overcome drug resistance by providing alternative treatment options.

papers.title: Design and Synthesis of Novel Imidazo[4,5-c]pyridine Derivatives, Evaluation of Their Activity Against Hepatitis C Virus and In Silico Prediction of Their Binding Mode to NS4B Protein.
papers.abstract: A series of suitably substituted imidazo[4,5-c]pyridine derivatives was synthesized, starting from 4-amino-2-chloropyridine, and their antiviral activity against hepatitis C virus (HCV) genotype 1b (GT1b) was evaluated in Huh5.2 replicon cells. Some of the N3-substituted analogs exhibited potent anti-HCV activity (EC50 = 6.61-47.24 μM), as determined by assessing their individual effects on viral RNA replication and protein expression levels. Among these, the most promising compound 30b demonstrated synergistic antiviral activity when combined with clinically approved direct-acting anti-HCV th
papers.summary: This paper describes the design and synthesis of new imidazo[4,5-c]pyridine derivatives that show activity against hepatitis C virus (HCV) genotype 1b, with the most promising compound (30b) exhibiting synergistic effects when combined with an approved anti-HCV drug. The compounds were tested in cell-based replicon assays and their binding to the viral NS4B protein was predicted computationally.

papers.title: Phytochemical profiling, network pharmacology, and molecular dynamics simulations of Sinularia levi metabolites as potential α-glucosidase inhibitors in metabolic syndrome.
papers.abstract: This study has investigated the phytochemical composition and therapeutic potential of the Red Sea soft coral Sinularia levi. Five compounds (1-5) comprising three glyceryl derivatives, a ceramide, and a diterpene were isolated and characterized. To elucidate their role in metabolic syndrome, an integrated network pharmacology and structure-based modeling workflow was employed. This approach identified 45 shared targets between S. levi metabolites and disease proteins, clustered into inflammatory and metabolic nodes, with α-glucosidase (GAA) emerging as a primary target. Molecular docking agai
papers.summary: This study identifies compounds from the Red Sea soft coral *Sinularia levi* that may inhibit α-glucosidase, a key target in metabolic syndrome. Using network pharmacology and molecular dynamics simulations, the authors found that a diterpene compound showed strong binding to α-glucosidase, suggesting potential as a natural therapeutic agent.

papers.title: Cryo-EM structure of the complex between transglutaminase 2 and the 45 kDa domain of fibronectin.
papers.abstract: Fibronectin (FN) and transglutaminase 2 (TG2) engage in a high-affinity interaction. This interaction affects anchoring, migration and survival of cells, and high cellular expression of TG2 is associated with metastasis potential of cancers. Previous work has mapped the interaction to involve the N-terminal part of TG2 and an N-terminally located 45 kDa domain of FN (FN45). In particular, the I8 module of FN45 is reported to be necessary and sufficient for TG2 binding. Here, we present the cryo-EM structure of a complex between human TG2 and FN45 at 2.8 Å resolution. In the structure, the dist
papers.summary: This study presents the first high-resolution cryo-EM structure of the complex between transglutaminase 2 (TG2) and the 45 kDa domain of fibronectin (FN45), revealing the molecular details of their interaction. The structure shows how the I8 module of FN45 binds to the N-terminal part of TG2, providing insights into how this interaction influences cell adhesion, migration, and cancer metastasis.

papers.title: Rational engineering of the Arabidopsis thaliana plant cysteine oxidase 4 active site can reduce biochemical activity and improve submergence tolerance.
papers.abstract: The increase in flooding events due to climate change is having devastating effects on food security. Plants can respond to submergence by reducing their energy requirements and switching their metabolism from oxidative phosphorylation to fermentation. This response is promoted by group VII ethylene response factors (ERF-VIIs) whose stability is dependent on oxygen availability; in normoxia, plant cysteine oxidase (PCO) enzymes catalyze oxidation of ERF-VII N-terminal Cys residues to trigger ERF-VII degradation via the Cys/Arg-N-degron pathway. In hypoxia, reduced PCO activity prevents ERF-VII
papers.summary: This study shows that rationally engineering the active site of the plant cysteine oxidase 4 (PCO4) enzyme can reduce its biochemical activity, which in turn improves submergence tolerance in plants. By slowing the oxygen-dependent degradation of group VII ethylene response factors (ERF-VIIs), the engineered PCO4 helps plants maintain stress responses under flooding conditions. This work offers a potential strategy for developing flood-tolerant crops to address climate change impacts on food security.

papers.title: ProtPen combines sequence- and structure-based approaches to facilitate protein function predictions on a proteome-wide scale.
papers.abstract: Proteins of unknown function represent a significant gap in our understanding of biological processes, encompassing large portions of the proteomes of many organisms, especially prokaryotes. Addressing this gap is critical to understanding the biology and pathogenicity of such organisms. We introduce ProtPen, an open-source pipeline that facilitates protein function prediction by combining eggNOG-mapper for sequence-based annotation with Foldseek for rapid structural similarity searches using AlphaFold-predicted protein structures. Annotation results from both tools are merged and enriched wit
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: ProtPen is a new open-source pipeline that combines sequence-based annotation (eggNOG-mapper) with rapid structural similarity searches (Foldseek) using AlphaFold-predicted structures to predict functions of unknown proteins across entire proteomes. This approach helps close the gap in understanding biological processes, especially for prokaryotes with many uncharacterized proteins.

papers.title: Benchmarking AI Protein Structure Predictors Reveals a Persistent Bias in Multi-State Proteins.
papers.abstract: Protein structure predictors achieve high single-state accuracy, but it remains unclear whether they can recover functionally relevant conformational ensembles or account for the presence of ligands and/or binding partners. Here, we benchmark AlphaFold3, Boltz-2, Chai-1, and BioEmu on four canonical multi-state proteins (Pf-MATE, LAO, SecA, and β 2 AR), quantifying state bias and sampling breadth against experimental reference structures. Models frequently default to a dominant state represented in the PDB; small-molecule ligands have weak or inconsistent effects, while large protein partners
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This study benchmarks four AI protein structure predictors (AlphaFold3, Boltz-2, Chai-1, and BioEmu) on multi-state proteins and finds they often default to a single dominant state from the PDB, failing to capture the full conformational ensembles. Small-molecule ligands had weak or inconsistent effects on predictions, while large protein partners showed some influence. The work highlights a persistent bias in current AI models toward single-state predictions, limiting their utility for studying functionally relevant protein dynamics.

papers.title: Targeting GALNT7 Disrupts the TAZ O-GalNAcylation Feedback Loop to Suppress Gallbladder Cancer Progression.
papers.abstract: Gallbladder cancer (GBC) is a lethal malignancy with limited therapeutic options and dismal prognosis. Identifying the factors driving GBC progression is crucial for developing potent preventive and therapeutic approaches. Here, using quantitative proteomics, we identify GALNT7 as the most significantly upregulated glycosyltransferase in GBC tissues, associated with adverse clinical outcomes. Mechanistically, GALNT7 physically interacts with TAZ and catalyzes O-GalNAcylation at Ser307, thereby inhibiting K48-linked ubiquitination through the recruitment of the deubiquitinase USP7 and stabilizi
papers.summary: This study identifies the glycosyltransferase GALNT7 as a key driver of gallbladder cancer progression. Using quantitative proteomics, the authors show that GALNT7 is upregulated in cancer tissues and stabilizes the TAZ protein by adding O-GalNAc sugars, which prevents its degradation and promotes tumor growth. Targeting GALNT7 disrupts this feedback loop and may represent a new therapeutic strategy for gallbladder cancer.

papers.title: Structural and Energetic Determinants of Monobody Recognition of Oncogenic KRAS Variants.
papers.abstract: Monobodies are engineered binding proteins that recognize extended protein surfaces and offer advantages over small-molecule inhibitors for targeting challenging KRAS oncoproteins. Monobody 12D4 exhibits high affinity and selectivity for the oncogenic KRAS(G12D) mutant, but the molecular determinants governing its recognition and the basis for its mutant selectivity remain poorly understood. Here, we combined molecular dynamics simulations and energy calculations to characterize the interactions between monobody 12D4 and WT KRAS as well as four clinically relevant oncogenic variants (G12C, G12
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This study uses computational methods to reveal how monobody 12D4 selectively binds the oncogenic KRAS(G12D) mutant over other KRAS variants, identifying key structural and energetic determinants of recognition. The findings provide a molecular basis for designing improved monobody-based inhibitors targeting challenging KRAS oncoproteins.

papers.title: Mechano-reprogramming of tumor-associated macrophages by engineering nanogel stiffness for suppression of tumor development.
papers.abstract: Nanoparticle mechanical properties, as critical factors in tumor targeting drug delivery, have recently been revealed to regulate cellular biological functions. While macroscopic mechanical stimuli are demonstrated to reprogram tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs), it is unclear how TAMs respond to microscopic stimuli from nanoparticles. Herein, we demonstrated that the stiff 15%NGs presented stronger interactions with integrin than the soft 2%NGs, promoting the polymerization of actin and the activation of yes-associated protein 1. Therefore, the pro-inflammatory phenotype of TAMs was enhanced
papers.summary: This study shows that the stiffness of nanogels used for drug delivery can reprogram tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) from a pro-tumor to a pro-inflammatory state. Stiffer nanogels interact more strongly with integrin on TAMs, activating actin polymerization and YAP1 signaling to enhance anti-tumor immune responses. This mechano-reprogramming approach offers a potential strategy to suppress tumor development by engineering nanoparticle mechanical properties.

papers.title: Subtype-selective binding of milk β-casomorphins across the opioid receptor family revealed by molecular docking and molecular dynamics simulation.
papers.abstract: β-casomorphins (BCMs) are opioid-like peptides produced during the digestion of β-casein in the gastrointestinal tract. They are also involved in other gastrointestinal and neurophysiological processes. Although the effect of BCM7 on the μ-opioid receptor (MOR) has been extensively studied, the molecular interaction mechanisms of other BCMs with different opioid receptors have not been elucidated. This study aimed to investigate the receptor-specific binding behavior of BCM3-BCM11 across the μ-, δ-, κ-, and nociceptin/orphanin FQ peptide (NOP) opioid receptors using structure-based computation
papers.summary: This study uses computational modeling to investigate how different milk-derived opioid peptides (β-casomorphins) bind to various opioid receptors. The findings reveal subtype-selective binding patterns, with BCM7 showing strongest preference for the μ-opioid receptor while other BCMs exhibit distinct binding behaviors across μ, δ, κ, and NOP receptors.

papers.title: Design and Engineering of an Artificial Bifunctional N-Deacetylase/N-Sulfotransferase for the Biosynthesis of N-Sulfated Heparosan.
papers.abstract: Heparin is a widely used anticoagulant, while its traditional animal-derived production faces significant challenges in safety and scalability. The active expression of the initiating-step bifunctional enzyme N-deacetylase/N-sulfotransferase (NDST) in bacteria remains a key bottleneck in heparin biosynthesis. Here, we firstly mined and characterized diverse polysaccharide N-deacetylases (NDases) in Escherichia coli and revealed several NDases that are active toward the deacetylation of heparosan. An artificial NDDr-RL-ST was designed and constructed by fusing an active NDase with a heterogenou
papers.summary: Researchers engineered an artificial bifunctional enzyme by fusing a bacterial N-deacetylase with a sulfotransferase, overcoming a key bottleneck in the bacterial biosynthesis of heparin. This breakthrough enables scalable, animal-free production of the widely used anticoagulant drug.

papers.title: Aromatic π-stacking stabilizes an α-helical SARS-CoV-2 MPER peptide that mimics the post-fusion spike and enables potent antiviral activity.
papers.abstract: Membrane fusion between SARS-CoV-2 and host cells is mediated by the spike protein and involves the membrane-proximal external region (MPER), a tryptophan-rich sequence implicated in viral entry. Here, we investigated the feasibility of MPER-derived peptides as potential antiviral agents and examined the structural determinants underlying their activity. A series of truncated MPER peptides was synthesized and evaluated for antiviral activity against SARS-CoV-2 in cellular assays, revealing potent inhibitory potency against different variants, with nanomolar IC50 values associated with sequence
papers.summary: This study shows that short peptides derived from the SARS-CoV-2 spike protein's membrane-proximal external region (MPER) can potently inhibit viral entry, with aromatic π-stacking interactions stabilizing their α-helical structure. The most effective peptides achieved nanomolar activity against multiple variants, suggesting a new class of antiviral agents that mimic the post-fusion spike conformation.

papers.title: Integrated Framework for Probing Multimodal Protein Foundation Models with Structure-Functional Interpretability Analysis in Detection of Allosteric Binding Sites.
papers.abstract: Allosteric regulation represents a fundamental mechanism of protein function, yet distinguishing allosteric from orthosteric protein binding sites remains a persistent computational challenge. While multimodal protein foundation models offer the potential to integrate complementary biological signals including sequence, structure, functional annotations, and conformational dynamics, their performance determinants in allosteric binding site detection remain poorly understood. We introduce a unified computational framework for profiling multimodal protein foundation models across distinct bindin
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This paper presents a computational framework that evaluates how multimodal protein foundation models (which integrate sequence, structure, and functional data) perform in detecting allosteric binding sites—a challenging task in protein biology. The framework helps interpret which biological signals are most important for distinguishing allosteric from orthosteric sites, potentially improving drug discovery efforts.

papers.title: Benchmarking AlphaFold and related deep learning approaches for modeling antibody and TCR antigen recognition.
papers.abstract: Determining the structural basis of antigen recognition by antibodies and T cell receptors (TCRs) provides critical insights into effective immune targeting and can inform design of biotherapeutics and vaccines. Accurate computational modeling of antibodies and TCRs in complex with their targets poses a major challenge for predictive methods, including AlphaFold, which is generally accurate for modeling protein complexes but has shown limited success for immune recognition. In this study we assessed the performance of AlphaFold2, AlphaFold3, increased sampling protocols, and related deep learn
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This study benchmarks AlphaFold2, AlphaFold3, and related deep learning methods for modeling antibody and T cell receptor (TCR) antigen recognition, finding that while AlphaFold is generally accurate for protein complexes, it has limited success for immune recognition. The authors assess increased sampling protocols and related approaches to evaluate their performance on this challenging problem.

papers.title: Pharmacophore-based virtual screening, molecular docking, MD simulation, MM-GBSA energy calculation and DFT analysis for the in silico discovery of a SaFtsZ inhibitor.
papers.abstract: The rapid emergence of antimicrobial resistance demands the discovery of new antibacterial targets and inhibitors. Staphylococcus aureus filamenting temperature-sensitive protein (SaFtsZ), an essential cytoskeletal protein involved in bacterial cytokinesis and Z-ring formation, has gained attention as a promising target for antibacterial drug discovery. In the present study, an integrated computational strategy involving pharmacophore mapping, molecular docking, molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, and density functional theory (DFT) analysis was employed to identify potential SaFtsZ inhibitor
papers.summary: This study uses computational methods to identify potential inhibitors of SaFtsZ, a protein essential for bacterial cell division in Staphylococcus aureus. By combining pharmacophore modeling, molecular docking, and simulations, the authors propose new candidate compounds that could serve as starting points for developing antibiotics against drug-resistant S. aureus.

papers.title: Recent Progress in the Development of Selective MAGL Modulators (2020-2026).
papers.abstract: Monoacylglycerol lipase (MAGL) is a key enzyme at the interface between the endocannabinoid system and lipid metabolism, playing a pivotal role in the hydrolysis of the endocannabinoid 2-arachidonoylglycerol and in the regulation of lipid mediators involved in inflammation, pain, neurodegeneration and cancer. Owing to its therapeutic relevance, MAGL has emerged as an attractive pharmacological target, stimulating extensive research efforts aimed at the development of potent and selective modulators of its activity. Advances in medicinal chemistry, together with the increasing application of in
papers.summary: This review covers recent progress (2020-2026) in developing selective modulators of monoacylglycerol lipase (MAGL), a key enzyme linking the endocannabinoid system to lipid metabolism and implicated in inflammation, pain, neurodegeneration, and cancer. It highlights advances in medicinal chemistry and the growing application of new technologies to create potent and selective MAGL-targeting compounds.

papers.title: Sesamin Ameliorates High-Sugar, High-Fat Diet-Induced Hepatic Dysfunction via CYP1A2-Mediated Regulation of Lipid Metabolism and Oxidative Stress.
papers.abstract: BackgroundHigh-sugar, high-fat diets (HSDFD) can lead to hepatic dysfunction, but the molecular mechanisms underlying protective compounds like sesamin remain unclear MethodsWe employed a multi-faceted approach combining in silico and in vivo techniques. Utilizing the AlphaFold database, we screened multi-species proteomes to identify potential sesamin targets. Molecular dynamics simulations and MM-PBSA analysis were used to characterize sesamin-protein interactions. In vivo studies included single-cell RNA sequencing of hepatocytes from HSDFD-fed mice. We validated our findings using cellular
papers.journal: Journal of cellular and molecular medicine
papers.summary: This study investigates how sesamin, a natural compound, protects against liver damage caused by high-sugar, high-fat diets. Using computational methods and mouse models, the researchers identified CYP1A2 as a key target through which sesamin regulates lipid metabolism and reduces oxidative stress in the liver.

papers.title: FlowDock: A unified flow-based framework for flexible protein-ligand docking and binding affinity prediction.
papers.abstract: Accurate prediction of protein-ligand complexes and binding affinity is critical for hit identification and optimization for structure-based drug design. Traditional docking simulates binding processes with searching algorithms guided by energy-scoring functions, which are quite computationally expensive and time-intensive. In contrast, deep learning approaches offer a cost-effective alternative, yet often generate conformations with limited physicochemical validity and fail to account for protein flexibility. To address these pitfalls, we propose FlowDock, a multitask framework enhanced by Ba
papers.summary: FlowDock is a new deep learning framework that simultaneously predicts protein-ligand docking poses and binding affinity, overcoming limitations of traditional methods by accounting for protein flexibility and generating more physically valid conformations. It offers a faster, cost-effective alternative to conventional energy-based docking while improving accuracy through a unified flow-based multitask approach.

papers.title: Molecular glue degraders: Rational design, specificity engineering, and advanced delivery.
papers.abstract: Molecular glue degraders (MGDs) have emerged as a transformative modality in the field of targeted protein degradation (TPD), enabling the selective elimination of disease-relevant proteins, including those traditionally considered undruggable. Unlike bifunctional proteolysis-targeting chimeras (PROTACs), MGDs operate through monovalent architectures that induce protein-protein interactions (PPIs) between E3 ligases and neosubstrates, offering advantages in chemical simplicity, cell permeability, and target scope. However, MGD discovery remains serendipitously, and a translational framework th
papers.summary: This review discusses molecular glue degraders (MGDs), a promising class of drugs that work by inducing interactions between E3 ligases and target proteins to trigger their degradation, offering advantages over traditional PROTACs in simplicity and cell permeability. The authors highlight progress in rational design, specificity engineering, and delivery strategies for MGDs, while noting that their discovery remains largely serendipitous and a translational framework is still needed.

papers.title: In Silico Rational Design of Novel 1,3,4-Oxadiazole Thioether Derivatives for the Discovery of Xanthomonas oryzae Inhibitors.
papers.abstract: A series of newly designed 1,3,4-oxadiazole derivatives demonstrated highly effective inhibitors against Xanthomonas oryzae pv. oryzae (Xoo) and exhibited significant antiviral and anti-biofilm properties. To further explore the structure-activity relationships of these compounds and develop novel molecules with higher activity, this study systematically analyzed the relationship between the structure and activity of a series of 1,3,4-oxadiazole compounds using computational methods including 3D-QSAR, molecular docking, and molecular dynamics simulations. Based on a dataset containing 39 compo
papers.summary: This study used computational methods like 3D-QSAR, molecular docking, and molecular dynamics simulations to design novel 1,3,4-oxadiazole thioether derivatives that inhibit Xanthomonas oryzae, a bacterial pathogen affecting rice. The designed compounds showed promising antibacterial, antiviral, and anti-biofilm activities, providing a rational approach for developing new agrochemicals.

papers.title: De Novo Synthesis in Cancer Immunotherapy: Applications and Prospects.
papers.abstract: De novo protein design enables the creation of proteins with entirely novel structures and biological functions from scratch, offering a promising new paradigm for cancer immunotherapy. Emerging methodologies are integrating principles of rational design, including specificity, modularity, and adaptability, into the development process from the outset. This innovative strategy holds the potential to advance how people understand and intervene against malignancy, through the strategic utilization of immune checkpoint regulation, cytokine engineering, cellular therapy, and signaling pathway remo
papers.summary: This review discusses how de novo protein design—creating proteins with entirely new structures and functions from scratch—can be applied to cancer immunotherapy. It highlights emerging rational design principles like specificity and modularity that are being integrated into therapies targeting immune checkpoints, cytokines, and cell signaling. The approach offers a promising new paradigm for developing more effective and adaptable cancer treatments.

papers.title: Rechargeable N-Halamine Hydrogel Paint Coating Based on Upcycled Sesame Meal Protein for Preventing Cross-Contamination on Food-Contact Surfaces.
papers.abstract: Sesame meal protein isolate (SPI) derived from sesame meal was utilized as an upcycled plant protein source to develop N-halamine structure-based antimicrobial coating system to prevent cross-contamination for fresh food produce. The coating matrix was formulated using SPI, tannic acid (TA), cellulose nanocrystal (CNC), and sodium hypochlorite (NaOCl). The N-halamine structure maintained a stable active chlorine content of approximately 5 µmol/cm2 and enabled repeated recharging to the initial level on the coated model food-contact surface, stainless steel (SS). The stability of the coated sur
papers.summary: This paper describes a new antimicrobial coating made from upcycled sesame meal protein that can be recharged with chlorine to kill bacteria on food-contact surfaces like stainless steel. The coating maintains stable active chlorine levels and can be recharged repeatedly, offering a sustainable way to prevent cross-contamination on fresh produce.

papers.title: Investigating and validating the molecular mechanisms of lipid metabolism regulation in myasthenia gravis development based on single-cell, bulk transcriptomics, and RT-qPCR.
papers.abstract: Myasthenia gravis (MG) is a complex autoimmune neuromuscular disorder, and the role of lipid metabolism dysregulation in MG pathogenesis remains unclear. This study aimed to investigate the molecular mechanisms of lipid metabolism regulation in myasthenia gravis development by integrating single-cell and bulk transcriptomic data. This study analyzed bulk RNA sequencing data from the GSE85452 dataset (13 MG patients and 12 healthy controls) and single-cell RNA sequencing data from the GSE227835 dataset (10 MG patients and 10 healthy controls). Differential expression analysis was performed, and
papers.summary: This study investigates how lipid metabolism dysregulation contributes to myasthenia gravis (MG) by analyzing single-cell and bulk transcriptomic data from MG patients and healthy controls. The authors identify key molecular mechanisms and validate findings with RT-qPCR, suggesting lipid metabolism plays a role in MG pathogenesis.

papers.title: Correction: Unraveling the molecular basis of snake venom nerve growth factor: human TrkA recognition through molecular dynamics simulation and comparison with human nerve growth factor.
papers.abstract: [This corrects the article DOI: 10.3389/fbinf.2025.1674791.].
papers.summary: This is a correction notice for a previously published article on snake venom nerve growth factor. It does not contain new scientific findings.

papers.title: Molecular insights into the functional mechanism of phosphoserine phosphatase SerB2 in Mycobacterium tuberculosis.
papers.abstract: Mycobacterium tuberculosis (Mtb) phosphoserine phosphatase SerB2 is a key enzyme essential for bacterial survival and virulence, catalyzing the final step in the L-serine biosynthesis pathway. SerB2 is an attractive drug target due to its role in immune evasion by Mtb. However, the functional mechanism of Mtb SerB2 remains poorly understood. Here, we measured the hydrodynamic radius of monomeric SerB2 to be 5.4 ± 0.3 nm. Based on an AlphaFold2-predicted structural model, molecular docking was performed and site-specific mutations were carried out. Mutants D185A, D187A, E194A, S226A, R230A or K
papers.summary: This study characterizes the phosphoserine phosphatase SerB2 from Mycobacterium tuberculosis, a key enzyme in L-serine biosynthesis and a potential drug target. Using AlphaFold2 structural predictions, molecular docking, and mutagenesis, the authors identify critical residues (D185, D187, E194, S226, R230, K) involved in its function, providing molecular insights into its mechanism.

papers.title: Elucidating the dual regulation of apoptosis and migration by Ajugasterone C in AGS cells via the PI3K/AKT pathway.
papers.abstract: This study aimed to investigate the effects of Ajugasterone C (AC), a primary phytochemical of Ajuga decumbens Thunb., on human gastric cancer AGS cells and to elucidate the underlying mechanisms.
papers.summary: This study shows that Ajugasterone C, a compound from the plant Ajuga decumbens, can both induce apoptosis (cell death) and inhibit migration in gastric cancer cells by regulating the PI3K/AKT signaling pathway. These findings suggest AC has potential as a therapeutic agent for gastric cancer.

papers.title: Integrated bulk, single-cell, and spatial transcriptomic analyses prioritize NOTCH1 as a candidate gene associated with neurovascular and immune-related alterations in Parkinson's disease.
papers.abstract: Parkinson's disease (PD) is classically defined by dopaminergic neurodegeneration in the substantia nigra, yet how immune activation is linked to neurovascular dysfunction in the diseased brain remains incompletely understood.
papers.summary: This study integrates bulk, single-cell, and spatial transcriptomic data to identify NOTCH1 as a key gene linking neurovascular and immune alterations in Parkinson's disease. The findings suggest that NOTCH1 may play a role in the interplay between blood vessel dysfunction and immune activation in the brain, offering a potential target for future therapeutic strategies.

papers.title: Anti-amyloid nanobody-Fc fusion protein drives potent amyloid clearance through microglial recruitment.
papers.abstract: Anti-amyloid beta (Aβ) monoclonal antibodies are effective at lowering amyloid in Alzheimer's disease (AD). However, whether Fc-mediated effector function is absolutely required for efficacy is not completely understood. This is important for optimizing therapeutic efficacy and mitigating side effects such as amyloid-related imaging abnormalities (ARIA). Antibodies lacking Fc effector function, like single-domain antibodies (nanobodies), offer a unique tool to dissect these mechanisms, as their small size facilitates blood-brain barrier (BBB) penetration and allows Fc-mediated functions to be
papers.summary: This study uses nanobody-Fc fusion proteins to show that Fc-mediated effector function is crucial for amyloid clearance in Alzheimer's disease, as the nanobody alone cannot clear amyloid without recruiting microglia. The findings help clarify the mechanism of antibody-based therapies and may inform strategies to reduce side effects like ARIA.

papers.title: Rational design 2.0: transitioning from static structural biology to computational prioritization and iterative vaccine optimization for RSV.
papers.abstract: Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) is a major cause of severe lower respiratory tract disease (LRTD) in infants and older adults worldwide. Although vaccines based on the fusion (F) protein have shown progress, their efficacy remains limited by antigenic instability and viral evolution. The metastable transition of the F protein between prefusion (preF) and postF conformations critically determines the exposure of neutralizing epitopes, with most potent antibodies targeting preF-specific sites. In addition, the glycosylated G protein contributes to immune evasion through glycan shielding and CX
papers.summary: This review discusses the transition from static structural biology to computational prioritization and iterative vaccine optimization for RSV, focusing on the metastable F protein and glycosylated G protein as key targets. It highlights how computational methods can help overcome challenges like antigenic instability and immune evasion to design more effective vaccines.

papers.title: Structural homology of HHV-6B epitopes as candidates for molecular mimicry triggers of the onset type one diabetes mellitus.
papers.abstract: To study whether HHV-6B can act as a molecular mimic to trigger onset of Type One Diabetes Mellitus (T1DM) by assessing structural and binding similarities between HHV-6B derived epitopes and T1DM autoantigen derived epitopes.
papers.summary: This study investigates whether human herpesvirus 6B (HHV-6B) can trigger type 1 diabetes through molecular mimicry, by comparing the structural and binding similarities between viral epitopes and diabetes-related autoantigens. The findings suggest that certain HHV-6B epitopes closely resemble T1DM autoantigens, supporting a potential viral trigger mechanism.

papers.title: Discovery of Galangin Derivatives as a Potential T-cell Leukemia Virus 1 Protease Inhibitor Through Chemoinformatics Approaches
papers.summary: This study used computational methods to identify galangin derivatives as potential inhibitors of the T-cell leukemia virus 1 protease. The findings suggest these compounds could serve as leads for developing new antiviral therapies against this virus.

papers.title: p70S6K as a Potential Anti-COVID-19 Target: Insights from Wet Bench and In Silico Studies
papers.abstract: The onset of SARS-CoV-2 infection in 2019 sparked a global COVID-19 pandemic. This infection is marked by a significant rise in both viral and host kinase activity. Our primary objective was to identify a pivotal host kinase essential for COVID-19 infection and the associated phenomenon of the cytokine storm, which may lead to long-term COVID-19 complications irrespective of viral genetic variations. To achieve this, our study tracked kinase phosphorylation dynamics in RAW264.7 macrophages following SPIKE transfection over time. Among the kinases surveyed, p70S6K (RPS6KB1) exhibited a 3.5-fold
papers.summary: This study identifies the host kinase p70S6K as a potential therapeutic target for COVID-19 by showing its 3.5-fold activation in macrophages following SPIKE transfection, suggesting it plays a key role in infection and cytokine storm. The combination of wet lab and computational analyses highlights p70S6K as a promising target independent of viral mutations.
